mapQuestions.question: Which continent is Tanzania on?
mapQuestions.answer: Africa, on the east coast. Tanzania is right below Kenya and faces the Indian Ocean 🌍

mapQuestions.question: What is the capital of Tanzania?
mapQuestions.answer: Dodoma (officially), but Dar es Salaam on the coast is much bigger and is the real economic capital. Most government work has been slowly moving inland to Dodoma since 1973.

mapQuestions.question: What languages does Tanzania speak?
mapQuestions.answer: Swahili is the national language and the first language of most Tanzanians. English is used in business and higher education. There are also 120+ local languages.

mapQuestions.question: What is the highest mountain in Africa?
mapQuestions.answer: Kilimanjaro, 5,895 metres, in northern Tanzania. It is the highest free-standing mountain in the world (it doesn't sit in a range), and you can walk to the top without climbing gear.

mapQuestions.question: Where is the Serengeti?
mapQuestions.answer: In northern Tanzania, joined onto Kenya's Maasai Mara. The Serengeti is the bigger half of the great wildebeest migration ecosystem.

foods.name: Ugali
foods.description: Stiff white maize porridge eaten with your right hand, dipped into stews. The Tanzanian (and East African) staple.

foods.name: Nyama choma
foods.description: Grilled meat (goat, beef or chicken) over hot coals, served with kachumbari salad and ugali. Saturday lunch with friends.

foods.name: Pweza wa nazi
foods.description: Octopus simmered in a rich coconut and tamarind curry. A Swahili-coast classic, especially in Zanzibar.

foods.name: Chapati
foods.description: Soft layered flatbread brought by Indian traders centuries ago, now totally Tanzanian. Eaten with stews or alongside chai for breakfast.

foods.name: Mishkaki
foods.description: Beef skewers marinated in spices and grilled on streetside coals at night. The Swahili-coast street snack.

foods.name: Mtori
foods.description: A creamy banana-and-beef soup from the Chagga people around Kilimanjaro. Cooking bananas (plantains), not sweet bananas.

geoMatches.description: The biggest city, on the Indian Ocean coast. Busy port, mix of Swahili, Arab and Indian influences, lively markets.
The vast northern savanna. Bigger half of the Great Migration, with millions of wildebeest and zebras crossing it each year.
Africa's highest mountain, 5,895 m, with five climate zones from rainforest to glacier in one walk up.
A 600-metre deep volcanic crater holding one of the densest concentrations of wildlife on Earth, including the elusive black rhino.
An Indian Ocean island with white-sand beaches, spice farms, dhow sailboats and a UNESCO-listed Stone Town.
The world's second-deepest and second-oldest lake, on Tanzania's western border. Home to chimpanzees in the Mahale forests above it.

senses.smell: Cloves and cardamom in Zanzibar's spice markets, woodsmoke from cooking fires at dusk, salty Indian Ocean breeze, hot earth after the first rains, fresh chapati frying…
senses.hear: Swahili greetings everywhere ("Jambo!" "Karibu!"), Maasai songs across the savanna, lions roaring at night, the call to prayer in Zanzibar, dhow sails flapping…
senses.taste: Coconut curry octopus, smoky nyama choma, sweet chai with cardamom, fresh mango from a roadside vendor, soft ugali scooped through stew…
senses.feel: Warm equatorial sun, fine white sand on Zanzibar beaches, the chill of a high Kilimanjaro morning, the bumpy seat of a safari truck, the soft cotton of a Maasai shuka…
senses.see: What is the most incredible thing right in front of you right now…

stories.question: How do you walk up the highest mountain in Africa?
stories.body: Kilimanjaro is 5,895 m tall, BUT unlike most mountains its size, you don't need ropes, ice axes or technical climbing to get to the top. Trails wind up the slopes from rainforest at the base, through bamboo, then moorland with weird giant lobelia plants, then alpine desert, and finally onto the snow and glacier of the summit. A typical climb takes 5 to 8 days. The big challenge is altitude: at the summit there is only half the oxygen of sea level, and altitude sickness can hit even fit teenagers. Local guides and porters (almost all Tanzanian) lead every climb. The climb is one of Tanzania's biggest employers.

stories.question: What is the Great Migration?
stories.body: Every year, around 1.5 million wildebeest, 200,000 zebras and 500,000 gazelles walk a giant circle following the rains and fresh grass: north into Kenya's Maasai Mara in July to October, then back south into the Tanzanian Serengeti to give birth in February. The Serengeti is the bigger half of the loop. In calving season, half a million baby wildebeest are born in just a few weeks, all in the same patch of grass, all watched closely by waiting lions, cheetahs and hyenas. The Migration has been happening like this for tens of thousands of years.

stories.question: Why is Zanzibar called the "Spice Island"?
stories.body: Zanzibar grows cloves, nutmeg, cinnamon, cardamom, vanilla, pepper, ginger and turmeric, all on small farms that visitors can tour. The spice trade made the island fabulously rich from the 1700s onwards: Arab, Indian, Portuguese and African merchants all met here. Stone Town, the old capital, is a maze of narrow streets, carved wooden doors and Swahili palaces, now a UNESCO World Heritage site. Today most of the spice on a Zanzibar farm is grown for tourists rather than for global export, but the smell of clove trees as you walk past is unmistakable.

stories.question: What is Ngorongoro Crater?
stories.body: Ngorongoro is the world's biggest unbroken volcanic caldera, 600 metres deep and 20 km across. It formed when a giant volcano collapsed in on itself about 2.5 million years ago. Inside the crater walls is a self-contained ecosystem with about 25,000 large animals: lions, elephants, buffalo, hippos, wildebeest, zebras, and one of Africa's last black rhino populations (about 50 individuals). The Maasai live and graze cattle on the crater rim, and tourist trucks drive down a steep track each morning. Standing on the rim and looking down feels like looking into a natural world zoo, except entirely wild.

stories.question: Where did humans come from?
stories.body: Olduvai Gorge, on the edge of the Serengeti, has been called "the cradle of humankind". In the 1950s and 60s the British-Kenyan archaeologists Louis and Mary Leakey dug up some of the earliest human fossils ever found here, including a 1.8-million-year-old skull from a human ancestor called Paranthropus. Stone tools 2.6 million years old were found in the same layers. Today most scientists agree that modern humans (us, Homo sapiens) all evolved in East Africa around 300,000 years ago, walked out across the world, and the rest is history. Standing in Olduvai Gorge feels like standing at the start of everything.
stories.thinkingQuestion: Every human on Earth's family tree eventually reaches East Africa. Does that change how you think about where you "come from"?

convoQuestions.question: Best food in Tanzania so far?
Have you said "asante" or "jambo" to anyone? How did they react?
Savanna, mountain, beach, city, which has felt the most "Tanzania"?
If you could see one Tanzanian animal up close, which would you pick?
How does a sky full of Serengeti stars compare to a sky back home?
Olduvai Gorge is where some of the oldest human fossils have been found. How would you feel about standing there?

wordSearch.hint: Tanzanian foods, places and things to spot.

animals.name: Lion
animals.description: The Serengeti has the largest lion population on Earth. Whole prides on rocky kopjes at dawn.

animals.name: African elephant
animals.description: Huge herds in Tarangire National Park, especially in the dry season around the baobabs.

animals.name: Plains zebra
animals.description: In huge mixed herds with wildebeest during the Great Migration through the Serengeti.

animals.name: Maasai giraffe
animals.description: The national animal of Tanzania, browsing acacia trees on the savanna.

animals.name: Hippopotamus
animals.description: Crowded pools in the Serengeti and Selous, snorting and yawning at dusk.

animals.name: Black rhino
animals.description: Critically endangered. Ngorongoro Crater holds one of the last viable Tanzanian populations.

animals.name: Chimpanzee
animals.description: In the Mahale and Gombe forests on the shores of Lake Tanganyika. Jane Goodall studied them here.
